narrative: I'm sorry to share that your law school application was rejected because our predictive model estimated a 42.8% chance that you might fail the bar exam on your first try, which is above the threshold we use for admissions decisions. This prediction is based on a careful look at your academic history, test scores, family background, and other details compared to thousands of past students. You ranked in the 2nd decile for your Year 1 school grades and a very low 1st decile for Year 3, which are notably below the typical student's mid-range performance around the 5th or 6th decile. Your LSAT score of 35 and undergraduate GPA of 2.90 also fell short of the averages seen in students who usually pass the bar, around 36.7 and 3.21 respectively. Your upper-middle family income bracket is solid but didn't outweigh these academic signals, while your background as a white female planning full-time studies aligns closely with the majority of applicants who still succeed. When the model weighed all this, your Year 3 decile ranking had the strongest influence, pushing the prediction toward a higher risk of bar failure more than any other factor. Right behind it, your Year 1 decile played a big role in the same direction, followed by family income, LSAT score, undergraduate GPA, racial background, gender, and full-time status—all contributing somewhat toward that concerning outcome, though to lesser degrees. In essence, your early and later school rankings stood out as the top concerns because they signal potential challenges in sustaining high performance, unlike most admitted students whose grades place them solidly in the middle or higher. This isn't a judgment on your potential, but a data-driven flag based on patterns where lower deciles often correlate with bar struggles. Looking ahead, you could realistically retake the LSAT to boost that score closer to or above average, as it's a key factor you can directly improve with preparation. Strengthening any future academic records, like graduate-level coursework, might also help demonstrate growth beyond your past deciles and GPA. Consider targeted bar prep strategies early on, drawing from resources that have helped students with similar starting points succeed. With focused effort on these areas, you could shift future predictions in your favor and reapply stronger. We're rooting for you to turn this around.
